perturbation.original_text: 9. The undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 will continue in force [indefinitely][for [insert number] years from the date of this Agreement].
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 will continue in force for 1 year from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This modification shortens the duration of confidentiality to 1 year, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that may require longer protection periods to maintain trade secret status. This is especially relevant if the Confidential Information constitutes a trade secret under the laws of England and Wales, where the contract is based.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 requires reasonable steps to be taken to keep information secret in order to be classified as a trade secret. A one-year confidentiality period may be deemed insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, potentially rendering the information no longer legally protected as such under UK law. The modified text contradicts the implied requirement to provide a reasonable protection period for maintaining trade secret status.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text shortens the confidentiality period to one year, potentially contradicting the implied requirement to provide a reasonable protection period for maintaining trade secret status under the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018. The Law Explanation asserts that a one-year period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, which aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' where time-sensitive obligations do not align with legal requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018,' which, when combined with the Perturbation and Law explanations, suggests the modification weakens the legally required protection for trade secrets, making the terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 7. The Recipient will, on request from the other party, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party to the Recipient and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. The Recipient will, on request from the other party, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party to the Recipient, but may retain one archival copy for legal compliance purposes.
perturbation.explanation: While seemingly reasonable, the addition of retaining 'one archival copy for legal compliance purposes' could conflict with GDPR (if the Confidential Information contains personal data) if the retention period and purpose are not clearly defined and compliant with data minimization principles. The modified text may violate regulations about data retention.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(e) of GDPR requires that personal data is kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed.  Retaining an archival copy 'for legal compliance purposes' without specifying the legitimate purpose and retention period could violate the data minimization and storage limitation principles of GDPR if the confidential information contains personal data and that data is kept longer than necessary.  The changed text introduces ambiguity and a potential violation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potential violation of GDPR's data minimization principle. While the original text required the recipient to return all confidential information, the changed text allows retaining 'one archival copy for legal compliance purposes.' Article 5 of GDPR, as indicated in the scraped snippet, mandates that personal data be 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed.' Retaining a copy for legal compliance without specifying the purpose or retention period directly contradicts this principle. The change creates an ambiguity that could lead to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. The English Courts will have non-exclusive jurisdiction to deal with any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. Any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement shall be resolved solely through binding arbitration in a jurisdiction chosen by the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: While arbitration is generally permissible, dictating that the 'disclosing party' solely chooses the jurisdiction for arbitration could be deemed unconscionable or unfair, particularly if the chosen jurisdiction has no reasonable connection to the agreement or the parties. This could be challenged under the Arbitration Act 1996 in England.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 68 of the Arbitration Act 1996 allows a challenge to an arbitral award on the grounds of serious irregularity affecting the tribunal, the proceedings, or the award. The clause granting the disclosing party sole discretion to choose the arbitration jurisdiction could be viewed as a serious irregularity if it leads to demonstrable unfairness or bias, potentially undermining the integrity of the arbitration process and providing grounds for challenging the award under Section 68. The implication is that this clause could be seen as allowing one party to rig the process.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies English law and the jurisdiction of English courts. The changed text mandates binding arbitration in a jurisdiction chosen solely by the disclosing party. The Law Explanation states that this clause could be seen as allowing one party to rig the process, potentially undermining the integrity of the arbitration process. The Perturbation Explanation specifies that this is due to the fact that the chosen jurisdiction might have no reasonable connection to the agreement or the parties, causing unfairness and violating the Arbitration Act 1996 in England. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 are not helpful, the modification from court jurisdiction to arbitration jurisdiction selected unilaterally contradicts fundamental fairness principles and could be challenged under the Arbitration Act, therefore a legal contradiction exists based on the provided explanations.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advisers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence to the other party and who are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 3 above and this clause 4.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party secure and not to disclose it to any third party, regardless of whether they have a need to know or have obligations of confidentiality.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the exception for employees and professional advisors who need to know the information and are bound by confidentiality obligations. This is overly restrictive and could violate data protection laws or employment laws if it prevents the Recipient from sharing necessary information with employees for legitimate business purposes or for compliance with legal obligations such as reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6(1)(f) of GDPR allows for the processing of personal data if it is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject. Preventing the disclosure of confidential information (potentially containing personal data) even to employees for legitimate business purposes could be considered a violation if it infringes upon the legitimate interests of the Recipient or the employees and those interests are not overridden by other factors. For example, refusing to disclose information required for payroll purposes would be a violation. The amended text removes a vital exception.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies: the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child. Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. Member States may maintain or introduce more specific provisions to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation with regard to processing for compliance with points (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 by determining more precisely specific requirements for the processing and other measures to ensure lawful and fair processing including for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 1 The basis for the processing referred to in point (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 shall be laid down by: Union law; or Member State law to which the controller is subject. 2 The purpose of the processing shall be determined in that legal basis or, as regards the processing referred to in point (e) of paragraph 1, shall be necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller. 3 That legal basis may contain specific provisions to adapt the application of rules of this Regulation, inter alia: the general conditions governing the lawfulness of processing by the controller; the types of data which are subject to the processing; the data subjects concerned; the entities to, and the purposes for which, the personal data may be disclosed; the purpose limitation; storage periods; and processing operations and processing procedures, including measures to ensure lawful and fair processing such as those for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 4 The Union or the Member State law shall meet an objective of public interest and be proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued. Where the processing for a purpose other than that for which the personal data have been collected is not based on the data subject’s consent or on a Union or Member State law which constitutes a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard the objectives referred to in Article 23 (1), the controller shall, in order to ascertain whether processing for another purpose is compatible with the purpose for which the personal data are initially collected, take into account, inter alia: any link between the purposes for which the personal data have been collected and
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the exception that allows the Recipient to disclose confidential information to its employees and professional advisors under certain conditions. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this removal creates a contradiction with GDPR, specifically Article 6(1)(f), because it prevents the processing of personal data necessary for legitimate business interests. Scraped Law Snippet 2 supports this, as it states that 'processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party'. The modified text could therefore impede legitimate business operations requiring data processing and sharing, which contradicts GDPR.
